exemplar.title:
To what extent do social-cultural factors influence
workplace burnout?

detailedExemplarData.title:
To what extent do social-cultural factors influence
workplace burnout?

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Topic is communicated clearly with an identifiable purpose and scope (macro-, meso-, micro-levels).
Research question is explicitly stated and maintained throughout the essay.
A coherent literature-review methodology is applied with generally relevant sources cited.
weaknesses: Key constructs such as âsociocultural factorsâ remain under-defined, diluting analytical precision.
The research question is broad and spans multiple levels without tight analytical focus.
Methodology lacks justification for exclusive reliance on secondary sources and limited critical appraisal of source selection.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work consistently communicates its focus and purpose, situating the phenomenon of burnout within organisational psychology and maintaining reference to the stated research question. However, vagueness around core constructs and only a partially focused question prevent it from achieving the highest band. A criterion score of 4 reflects strong performance at the top of the adequate band while acknowledging these limitations.

strengths: Solid understanding of burnout theory (Maslach, JDâR) and cultural models (Hofstede), with accurate application to the argument.
Effective integration of empirical findings, notably the detailed treatment of Santiago et al. (2024).
Generally precise use of subject-specific terminology in meso- and micro-level discussions.
weaknesses: Reliance on non-academic sources (e.g., WebMD, HR websites) undermines source credibility.
Terminology is occasionally imprecise or undefined (e.g., âmotivationâ¦ having a negative mindsetâ).
Repetition of points across sections suggests uneven integration of sources.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The essay demonstrates clear and mostly accurate subject knowledge, applying terminology and concepts appropriately in most sections. Occasional superficiality and reliance on lower-rigour sources prevent a top-band rating. A score of 4 captures solid performance in Knowledge and Understanding while recognising these inconsistencies.

strengths: Draws on a diverse range of peer-reviewed studies, meta-analyses and theoretical works directly relevant to the research question.
Shows critical awareness of methodological limitations, notably correlational designs and causality constraints.
Organises analysis logically around macro-, meso-, and micro-levels, linking findings to practical intervention design.
weaknesses: Evaluative depth is unevenâmeta-analyses and case studies are often summarised without full critical appraisal.
Limited synthesis of contradictory evidence or exploration of alternative explanations.
Analysis sometimes remains descriptive, with few concrete strategies proposed to address identified research gaps.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the research base is generally relevant and the student demonstrates clear analysis with supported conclusions, the evaluation lacks the consistent depth and sophistication of higher bands. A score of 6 places the work in the upper mid-range of Critical Thinking, reflecting both its strengths and areas where deeper critical synthesis is needed.

strengths: Clear use of section headings (Introduction, Content â Macro/Meso/Micro, Conclusion) enhances navigability.
Logical three-level organisational framework is consistently maintained throughout the essay.
weaknesses: Absence of an explicit word count and inconsistent pagination detract from formal conventions.
Citation and formatting conventions are applied only partially, with non-standard markers used for dividers.
Layout considerations (e.g., page numbering, table of contents) are incomplete.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The structure of the essay follows expected conventions, but key formal presentation requirements are only partially met. A score of 2 reflects this mixed performance in Presentation, balancing clear organization against significant lapses in formatting and formal conventions.

strandSelections.reasoning:
Key psychology termsâburnout dimensions, depersonalisation, affective commitment, power distanceâare used and generally defined, indicating familiarity with disciplinary language.  Citations usually accompany terminology, supporting accuracy.

However, precision is not fully consistent; phrases such as âmotivationâ¦ having a negative mindsetâ blur conceptual boundaries, and some constructs (e.g., âdeep coping strategiesâ) are invoked without clear operational detail.  Occasional grammatical issues also interfere with technical clarity.

This mostly correct but uneven usage aligns with Band 3-4.  A score of 3 is appropriate because consistency falls short of the uppermost performance in this band.

The layout shows some attention to academic presentation: headings are used, citations and a reference list are provided, and the title appears at the start.  However, key formal requirements for an EEâexplicit word count, consistent page numbering, and fully standardised citation formattingâare absent or only partially met in the excerpt.

Typography is plain text with unusual divider markers ("{0}ââ") rather than conventional pagination or tables of contents, suggesting incomplete adherence to formatting conventions.

Because some but not all layout considerations are correctly applied, this aligns with Band 1-2.  A minimal score of 1 is awarded to reflect partial but limited compliance.

The student demonstrates solid knowledge of burnout theory (Maslach, JDâR), cultural dimensions (Hofstede), and organisational ethics.  Empirical findings are summarised accurately and applied to the argument at each level of analysis.  Sources are generally integrated to illustrate points rather than merely listed, showing understanding of their content.

There is occasional superficial treatment (e.g., equating âkaroshiâ directly with burnout without nuance) and some reliance on broad, non-academic sources, preventing the work from reaching the âconsistently excellentâ threshold.  Nonetheless, overall grasp of subject matter is clear and mostly accurate.

These characteristics fit Band 3-4; the stronger end of the band (score 4) is justified by the breadth and mostly appropriate application of research.

The essay employs a literature-review methodology, drawing on meta-analyses, large surveys and conceptual models (e.g., JD-R theory).  Sources span peer-reviewed journals and some web articles, indicating a moderate but not extensive range.  Citations are provided in APA-style and generally relevant to the line of argument.

Evidence of informed selection is mixed.  Key seminal work (Maslach, Hofstede) is sensibly included, yet reliance on secondary summaries (WebMD) and limited critical appraisal of study design suggest only partial discernment.  No primary data are collected, and methodological justification for a purely secondary approach is not articulated.

Overall, methodology is âmostly completeâ and âgenerally relevantâ, matching Band 3-4.  A score of 3 is assigned to acknowledge adequacy while recognising the limited depth and selectivity.

The student introduces the phenomenon of workplace burnout clearly, provides basic prevalence statistics and situates the discussion within organisational psychology.  The scope (macro-, meso-, micro-level sociocultural factors) is stated and revisited in the conclusion, giving the essay an identifiable purpose.

However, the explanation of the topic remains relatively general and descriptive; key constructs such as âsociocultural factorsâ are outlined but not sharply delimited, and the breadth of coverage tends to dilute focus.  Nevertheless, readers can understand what will be examined and why it matters, so communication is generally effective.

Given this clarity but only adequate precision, the work aligns with Band 3-4. A score of 4 is awarded to reflect performance at the top of the adequate band without fully reaching the consistently precise standard of the highest band.

The essay draws upon a reasonable variety of peer-reviewed journal articles, meta-analyses, and theoretical works.  Most sources are pertinent to sociocultural influences on burnout, and citations span different countries and organisational settings, showing effort to ensure relevance.

Nevertheless, some references (e.g., WebMD, popular press articles) are of limited academic rigour, and there is no evidence of primary data collection or systematic selection criteria.  Depth of engagement with individual studies is sometimes cursory.

Given that the research base is sizeable and generally relevant but not consistently of the highest academic quality or exhaustiveness, the strand fits comfortably in Band 7-9 (âgood research mostly relevantâ).  A score of 7 reflects the lower end of that band.

The research question âTo what extent do sociocultural factors influence workplace burnout?â is explicitly stated and referred to throughout the essay.  It is, however, very broad: it spans multiple levels (macro, meso, micro) and a wide variety of potential variables, making comprehensive treatment within 4,000 words difficult.

Because the question is only partially focused, the subsequent discussion often shifts between culture, ethical climate, personal demographics, and socio-economic status without a tight analytical thread.  The connection between the research question and the argument is present but not always tight, as evaluative depth varies across sections.

This places the work solidly in Band 3-4 (clearly stated but only partially focused).  A score of 3 reflects a mid-band performance rather than the stronger focus expected for a 4.

The essay follows standard EE structural conventions: title page, abstract substitute (introduction with RQ), organised sections (Introduction, Content subdivided into macro/meso/micro, Conclusion), and a reference list.  Logical progression within sections is generally clear, aiding reader navigation.

Minor structural weaknesses are evidentâsome repetition, uneven subsection lengths, and occasional digressionsâbut the overall framework is appropriate and complete.

These qualities match Band 3-4 (âclearly appropriate structure meeting all conventionsâ).  A score of 3 is chosen to reflect solid compliance while acknowledging room for tighter organisation.

The argument is organised around macro-, meso- and micro-level factors, providing a logical framework that is maintained through to the conclusion.  The student does attempt evaluationâquestioning the validity of meta-analytic methods and correlational designsâindicating some critical stance.

However, evaluative comments are often brief and do not lead to substantial re-interpretation of evidence or nuanced weighing of perspectives.  The conclusion largely summarises earlier points rather than synthesising them to build a critical position on âto what extentâ.

Overall, the discussion is reasoned and partly critical, satisfying Band 4-6 criteria.  A score of 5 recognises adequate but not consistently penetrating evaluation.

The student links empirical findings to the research question, explaining how cultural dimensions moderate JDâR relationships and how ethical climate affects burnout dimensions.  Some methodological critique is offered (e.g., limitations of correlational surveys and operational definitions), which strengthens analysis.

Yet, much of the discussion remains descriptive, with limited synthesis across studies and few attempts to weigh contradictory evidence or explore alternative explanations.  Causal inference is noted as problematic, but the implications of this limitation for the central claim are not fully developed.

The work therefore meets the descriptors for Band 4-6: clear analysis with mostly supported conclusions, but without the consistent depth and sophistication necessary for the higher bands.  A mid-band score of 5 is warranted.

annotations.comment: The title is clearly stated at the start of the report, fulfilling a key formal requirement and orienting the reader effectively to the investigationâs focus.
A word count is missing at the beginning of the document, which is a required element for compliance with extended essay formatting guidelines.
The introduction outlines broad prevalence statistics but does not delimit key constructs such as âsociocultural factors,â leaving the scope of analysis vague and diluting focus.
Definitions of burnout are compiled from multiple sources without reconciling conceptual differences or discussing measurement implications, reducing analytical coherence.

The 65% burnout statistic is drawn from a webâbased HR source rather than a peerâreviewed study; reliance on nonâacademic references weakens source credibility and depth of subject understanding.
The âkaroshiâ case study is evocative but extrapolated without discussing cultural specificity or generalisability, limiting the robustness of its application to broader contexts.
The summary statement on cultural dimensions reiterates earlier points without offering new insight or synthesis, reducing analytical progression.
The methodology section outlines a literatureâreview approach but lacks justification for why secondary analysis alone is sufficient, limiting transparency around informed method selection.
Discussion of macroâlevel concepts is descriptive and not consistently linked back to the central research question, weakening coherence between inquiry and analysis.
A lengthy summary of Hofstedeâs dimensions is presented without critically synthesising how each specifically moderates burnout, leading to superficial coverage of theoretical links.
The metaâanalysis by Rattrie et al. is cited but not critically appraised (e.g., inclusion criteria, heterogeneity), reducing the depth of evaluation of its relevance and limitations.
The Bansal (2019) study is introduced without detailing effect sizes or statistical significance, reducing the readerâs ability to assess result robustness.
The discussion of correlational limitations demonstrates critical awareness of causality constraints, strengthening the evaluation of methodological boundaries.
The clear âConclusionâ heading adheres to structural conventions and signals a shift to synthesising findings.
The conclusion restates macro, meso and micro impacts but stops short of directly answering âto what extent,â limiting the precision of the final synthesis.
The mention of deeper metaâanalysis is unaccompanied by a plan to address identified gaps, leaving methodological recommendations underdeveloped.
This passage largely repeats earlier macroâlevel discussion, suggesting uneven source integration and missed opportunity to consolidate related points.
The clear âContent â Mesoâ heading maintains a logical three-level framework, enhancing navigability and structural coherence.
The definition of meso-level factors is precise and supported by a relevant citation, demonstrating accurate use of subject-specific terminology and concepts.
The description of Santiago et al. (2024) is thorough, clearly defining key constructs (emotional exhaustion, depersonalisation, personal realisation) and linking them to empirical findings.
The discussion of affective commitmentâs moderating role ends mid-argument and fails to reconnect explicitly to the central research question, weakening focus.
The review of Huhtalaâs survey lacks evaluation of design features (e.g., sample representativeness, measurement bias), which would strengthen critique of research quality.
The observation about vague operational definitions highlights an important issue but stops short of offering concrete strategies for standardisation.
The critique of inconsistent measurement across studies is valid, but the argument would benefit from examples of standardized instruments to illustrate solutions.
Repetition of meso-level findings reduces analytical momentum; consolidating similar points would improve clarity and concision.
The âContent â Microâ heading preserves the organizational framework, guiding the reader through successive analytical levels.
Soaresâs exclusively female sample is noted but not critically examined for gender biases or generalisability, limiting evaluation of research relevance.
Use of socio-economic terminology and specific examples in the micro-level discussion demonstrates accurate and consistent application of subject-specific concepts.
Linking the layered analysis to practical intervention design shows effective synthesis and application of findings to real-world organisational practices.
